burial.plot_notes: Berlin Corner Cemetery, Berlin Corners VT — alongside Dr. Ira.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of Civil War Medal of Honor recipient
narrative_blocks.body: Emeline Hobart Evans was mother of Ira Hobart Evans — Civil War Medal of Honor recipient and later Speaker of the Texas House — and of three other surviving children. Of her seven total children, only three reached middle age: Mary Emeline who married Captain Thomas Blossom Yale, Edward P. (1842-1913 Civil War 10th Vermont Infantry severely wounded Cold Harbor), and Ira Hobart Evans (1844-1922). The two Civil War sons gave the family a doubled Vermont-regiment combat record from the same Berlin Vermont household.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost four of seven children before adulthood
narrative_blocks.body: Emeline and Dr. Ira Evans's seven children included four childhood deaths: Mary E. (1834-1837, age 3); Ira (1836-1838, age 2 — the first attempt at the Ira name that her later son would carry); J. Annette (1840-1842, age 2); and William Dwight (1849-1861, age 12). All four were buried at Berlin Corner Cemetery in Vermont alongside their parents — a sequential-grief family burial cluster.

narrative_blocks.header: Widowed 1852, moved family to Barre Vermont
narrative_blocks.body: After her husband Dr. Ira Evans died in 1852 at age forty-nine in Piermont New Hampshire, Emeline moved the family to Barre Vermont so her surviving sons could complete their education at Barre Academy — the pivot positioned both Edward and Ira Hobart Evans for their Civil War service paths. Emeline lived another forty-two years as widow, dying in 1894 around age eighty-three. She was born in 1811 in Berlin Vermont.

burial.plot_notes: Berlin Corner Cemetery, Berlin Corners VT — alongside Dr. Ira.

parents.father_research_notes: Evans family of Vershire VT. Not built — non-tree.

narrative_blocks.header: A country doctor who buried four young children
narrative_blocks.body: Ira Evans practiced as a country physician in Piermont, New Hampshire, where four of his seven children with Emeline died before the age of three. The three who lived to adulthood included Ira Hobart Evans, who won the Medal of Honor and became Speaker of the Texas House, and Edward, severely wounded at Cold Harbor in 1864.

burial.plot_notes: Berlin Corner Cemetery, Berlin Corners VT.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name carries her mother Emeline Hobart Evans's first name forward.

parents.father_research_notes: Yale family of Brighton/Monroe County NY. Not built — non-tree.

burial.plot_notes: Berlin Corner Cemetery, Berlin Corners VT.
Berlin Corner Cemetery, Berlin Corners VT.

narrative_blocks.header: Severely wounded at Cold Harbor, June 1864
narrative_blocks.body: Edward served with the 10th Vermont Volunteer Infantry — the same regiment his younger brother Ira Hobart Evans had enlisted in two years earlier. He was severely wounded at the Battle of Cold Harbor Virginia on June 1, 1864, and was transferred to the Veteran Reserve Corps for invalid duty.

military_service.unit: 10th Regiment Vermont Volunteer Infantry → Veteran Reserve Corps
military_service.notes: Severely wounded at the Battle of Cold Harbor, Virginia, June 1, 1864; transferred to Veteran Reserve Corps for invalid duty.

residence.note: North-Evans Chateau (LM063), now Austin Woman's Club.

burial.plot_notes: Berlin Corner Cemetery, Berlin Corners VT — Vermont burial after California death per his wishes.

sources.label: Texas State Historical Association — Evans, Ira Hobart

notable.notable_blurb: Civil War Medal of Honor; Texas House Speaker

narrative_blocks.header: Walked alone into the no-man's-land at Hatcher's Run
narrative_blocks.body: On April 2, 1865, when Gen. William Birney needed deserters questioned before a planned afternoon assault on Confederate works at Hatcher's Run, Captain Evans was the only staff officer to volunteer. He dismounted, passed alone over the ridge — the only Union soldier in view from Confederate lines — drew a sharp musketry fire, made the rifle pit, questioned the deserters, and returned through another shower of bullets to report.

narrative_blocks.header: Honor guard at Lincoln's funeral
narrative_blocks.body: Two weeks after his Hatcher's Run heroism, Evans was selected as one of the officers in President Abraham Lincoln's funeral cortège honor guard on April 17, 1865. He was twenty-one years old. He had risen from private to brevet major in three years.

narrative_blocks.header: Speaker of the Texas House at twenty-six
narrative_blocks.body: Elected Speaker of the Texas House of Representatives in 1870 — the youngest person ever to hold the office. Months later he opposed an August 1870 election law that postponed the next election from 1871 to 1872 in violation of the 1869 Constitution; the Republican caucus denounced him and voted to remove him from the speakership the following morning.

narrative_blocks.header: Funded a Black college's industrial-training building
narrative_blocks.body: Trustee of Tillotson College (now Huston-Tillotson University) 1881-1920 and board president 1909-1920, Evans donated $10,000 to construct the 1912 Evans Industrial Building — which trained Black students in construction skills and bears his name. The building stands today as a Texas Historical Site. He bequeathed an additional $10,000 for the college president's residence.

narrative_blocks.header: First cousin of Pampa Texas's Timothy Dwight Hobart
narrative_blocks.body: Wikipedia confirms what the Berlin Vermont genealogy shows — Ira's mother Emeline Hobart Evans and Timothy Dwight Hobart's father David Hobart were full siblings, children of Rev. James Hobart of Berlin Vermont. Two Berlin Vermont-born first cousins, both Texas pioneers, both buried back home. Timothy at Pampa Fairview, Ira at Berlin Corner.

cross_connections.display_label: First cousins; both Berlin Vermont-born Texas pioneers
Cousin Porter Kendrick commanded Michigan infantry as Civil War Major

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Hobart' honors his maternal grandparents — Berlin VT's first settled minister Rev. James Hobart and Betsey Perrin Hobart — and was carried forward into the next generation as his son Hobart Yale Evans's first name.

education.notes: Completed his education at Barre Academy after his mother moved the family to Barre VT following his father's 1852 death.

career.organization: Self / Texas ranch near Corpus Christi
career.notes: Lost investment through dishonesty of a partner.
Resigned January 31, 1868 angered by superiors he considered incompetent.

career.location: Eagle Pass and Corpus Christi, Texas

career.notes: Representing Western District of Texas; ran at urging of Republican gubernatorial candidate Edmund Jackson Davis 1869.

Elected Speaker 1870 at age 26 — youngest in Texas history. Removed by Republican caucus over August 1870 election law dispute (he opposed postponing 1871 election to 1872 as violating Constitution of 1869).

career.organization: Houston and Great Northern Railroad Company

career.notes: Secretary from 1874 merger of Houston & Great Northern with International Railroad. Director 1875-1908.

career.organization: New York and Texas Land Company, Limited
career.notes: Same NY&T Land Company where his cousin Timothy Dwight Hobart of Pampa worked as surveyor under E.A. Giraud 1882-86 then managed 1 million Panhandle acres from Mobeetie 1886-1903.
Trustee 1881-1920; board president 1909-1920. $10,000 donation to construct Evans Industrial Building (1912; Texas Historical Site, named for him); $10,000 bequest for college president's residence.

career.organization: Texas Society of the Sons of the American Revolution (SAR)
career.notes: Vermont SAR member from 1896 (Vermont Society #51, national #2751); founded TX Society and was first president.

military_service.unit: Co. B 10th VT Vol. Inf. → 9th US Colored Troops → 116th US Colored Troops → XXV Army Corps Adjutant
military_service.notes: Enlisted Private Co. B 10th Vermont Volunteer Infantry July 28, 1862. Commissioned 1st Lieutenant 9th US Colored Troops December 18, 1863. Promoted Captain commanding Co. B 116th US Colored Troops January 27, 1865. Brevet Major March 13, 1865 'for gallant and meritorious service'; assigned acting assistant adjutant general of XXV Army Corps, Army of the James. April 17, 1865 — officer in honor guard of Lincoln's funeral cortège. Mustered out February 7, 1867 at Louisville KY; provost marshal of Brownsville TX during Sheridan's occupation force.

institutions.institution_blurb: Trustee 1881-1920 and board president 1909-1920 of the Black Austin college; donor of the Evans Industrial Building.
President of the New York and Texas Land Company 1880-1906 — same firm that employed his cousin Timothy Dwight Hobart of Pampa.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: His Austin home, now the Austin Woman's Club; an Austin Historic Landmark.
1912 Tillotson College building constructed with his $10,000 donation and named for him; Texas Historical Site.

parents.father_research_notes: Hurlbut family of Upper Alton IL. Researched: this Hurlbut line appears unrelated to the New England Hurlbut cluster (HD0676-HD0694, I00153 Dr. James Hurlbut, X00422 Anne Wright Hurlbut etc.) already in the Hooker tree — Hooker-tree Hurlbuts are largely New England Connecticut and Vermont; Fannie's Hurlbuts are Illinois. Possible common surname-deep ancestor not investigated. Not built.

burial.plot_notes: Mount Hebron Cemetery, Montclair NJ — solo Hooker-tree connection, no CEM entry built per §0.4 threshold.

residence.note: 704 San Antonio Street; died at 1000 Winstead Lane.

burial.plot_notes: Alton Cemetery, Alton IL — alongside maternal grandmother Fannie Hurlbut. Buried November 15, 1951 (removal from Texas).

naming_inspiration.description: First name 'Hobart' carries forward through three generations the surname of his great-grandmother Betsey Perrin Hobart and his father Ira Hobart Evans's middle name.
Middle name 'Yale' carries his paternal aunt Mary Emeline Evans Yale's married surname forward — preserving the Yale family connection through her marriage to Capt. Thomas Blossom Yale.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Hurlbut' carries his mother Francese 'Fannie' Hurlbut Evans's maiden surname forward.

narrative_blocks.header: William died young
narrative_blocks.body: William died in 1861, aged about twelve.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Dwight' honors his maternal uncle Timothy Dwight Hobart (1826-1853), who died at twenty-seven while preparing at Andover for the ministry — himself named in honor of Yale's Timothy Dwight.

burial.plot_notes: Berlin Corner Cemetery, Berlin Corners VT — alongside Betsey.

sources.label: Vermont Genealogy — Biography of Rev. James Hobart

narrative_blocks.header: Berlin Vermont's first settled minister thirty-one years
narrative_blocks.body: Ordained November 7, 1798 as Berlin Congregational's first settled minister, James drafted the church's confession of faith, covenant, and articles of discipline himself. His 31-year pastorate ran to May 1829, when a mutual council dismissed him. He then spent twelve years preaching in New Hampshire and twenty more itinerating around Worcester, Berlin, and West Berlin Vermont.

narrative_blocks.header: Walked six miles and preached at ninety-five
narrative_blocks.body: Per his obituary, 'in the very last year of his life, his 96th, he was able to preach a pretty well connected discourse, and could walk 6 or 8 miles in a day.' Below ordinary height with erect carriage, great memory, and a strong distinct voice, James was described as 'self-denying, laborious and persevering, having quite a missionary spirit.'

narrative_blocks.header: Three daughters married Congregational ministers
narrative_blocks.body: Three of James and Betsey's daughters married Congregational ministers across New England: Pamelia married Rev. Rufus Child (Gilmanton NH then Berlin), Julia married Rev. Pliny Fisk Barnard (Richmond ME then Williamstown VT then Westminster VT), and Hannah married Rev. George Craven (Methodist, Danville VT). The Berlin pulpit's reach extended through the next generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Walked six miles a day preaching at ninety-six
narrative_blocks.body: In the ninety-sixth year of his life Rev. Hobart preached well-connected discourses and was able to walk six or eight miles in a day. Below average height, he stood erect and was blessed with a retentive memory, strong voice, and good delivery.

education.notes: Apprentice studies with Asa Burton of Thetford VT before preaching license 1795.

career.notes: Ordained November 7, 1798. Drew up church confession of faith, covenant, and articles of discipline. 31-year pastorate dismissed by mutual council May 1829.
Plymouth, Wentworth, Enfield, Alexandria, Bridgewater NH and near Portsmouth (12 years); then Worcester, Berlin, West Berlin VT (last 20 years).

burial.plot_notes: Berlin Corner Cemetery, Berlin Corners VT — FindAGrave 90950772.

sources.label: Vermont Genealogy — Biography of Rev. James Hobart

residence.note: Settled May 1789 from Hebron CT; farmed 1300 acres at Scott Hill/East Road intersection in East Road School District.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Established the 1300-acre East Road farmstead and built permanent buildings here after settling Berlin VT in 1789.

burial.plot_notes: Berlin Corner Cemetery, Berlin Corners VT.

narrative_blocks.header: Settled Berlin Vermont with collapsed-roof cabin
narrative_blocks.body: Zachariah served in the Revolutionary War with the 8th company of the 12th Connecticut militia, then in February-March 1789 led his family from Hebron Connecticut to Berlin Vermont — up the Connecticut River with two teams of oxen, switching from wheels to runners in Brookfield. They found the log cabin he had built the previous summer crushed under three or four feet of snow.

narrative_blocks.header: Founded a 1300-acre farm still in family
narrative_blocks.body: Zachariah's 1300 acres in the East Road School District of Berlin VT — at what became the southeast corner of East Road and Scott Hill Road — passed down through generations of Perrins. His son Porter, the first boy born in Berlin, built the house at Scott Hill and Airport Road in 1815 (still standing). A descendant Henry Perrin still lived in 'Perrin territory' along Scott Hill Road into the modern era.

cross_connections.display_label: Settled Vermont 1789 after cousin David Newton's 1777 migration

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Lived on the Perrin family farmstead from her arrival in Berlin in 1789 until her death in 1828.

burial.plot_notes: Berlin Corner Cemetery, Berlin Corners VT — alongside Zachariah.

narrative_blocks.header: Walked to Vermont with two oxen teams
narrative_blocks.body: In February or March 1789 Mary and Zachariah moved their family from Hebron Connecticut to Berlin Vermont — traveling up the Connecticut River with two teams of oxen, then up the White River, switching from wheels to runners at Brookfield, then over a blazed trail. They arrived to find their log cabin's roof had collapsed under three to four feet of snow.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost firstborn daughter Mary in 1798
narrative_blocks.body: Mary's eldest child — also named Mary, born in Hebron — died in 1798. Four months later another daughter was born and named Mary in her sister's memory. The second Mary later married Julius Yemans Dewey and was the mother of Admiral George Dewey, hero of Manila Bay 1898.

burialCemetery.name: Berlin Corner Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Berlin Corner Cemetery, Berlin Corners Vermont. Burial site of the Perrin-Hobart founding family of Berlin VT — Zachariah Perrin Esq. (X01832, Berlin VT 1789 founder, Rev War vet, d.1838 age 90), his wife Mary Talcott Perrin (T01387, d.1828), their daughter Betsey Perrin Hobart (TD0120, d.1876), her husband Rev. James Hobart (X02368, Berlin VT first settled minister, d.1862 age 95), their son David Hobart (TD0129, d.1901), and his wife Caroline Reed Hobart (X02370, d.1898). Three Perrin-Hobart generations. [pilot100] Backfilled with Evans/Yale Berlin VT generation: Dr. Ira Evans Sr. (X02383) and Emeline Hobart Evans (TD0124, daughter of Rev. James + Betsey Hobart); their seven children including 4 died young/childhood/infancy (Mary E., Ira Jr., J. Annette, William Dwight) and 3 adult-lifespan (Mary Emeline Evans Yale + her Civil War vet husband Capt. Thomas Blossom Yale 108th NY Inf, Edward P. Evans 10th VT Inf wounded Cold Harbor, and Civil War Medal of Honor recipient Ira Hobart Evans TD0163 who died San Diego CA 1922 and was returned to Berlin Corner per his wishes). Cemetery now spans four generations of Hobart-Evans-Yale family across ~150 years.
